input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware architecture comprises lower and upper chassis levels together with autonomy elements that include the sensor suite, actuation hardware and the on‑board electronic control units (ECU). The vehicle serves as a test platform for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) in a controlled test environment, continuously publishing vehicle state, projected driving path and nearby object tracks, which makes it a suitable candidate for digital‑twin replication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers three tightly coupled services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) presents real‑time vehicle state data, projected path and detected object tracks, and enables users to view, save, and generate performance reports; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm is migrated from the resource‑constrained on‑board ECU to a cloud‑based DT where it determines the lead vehicle, computes a desired speed based on inter‑vehicle distance, and returns the speed command to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable/disable ACC, set the desired speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command through the UI. The services target two user classes: operators (who monitor and control the ACC remotely) and developers (who use monitoring and data‑collection capabilities for troubleshooting and development).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation relies on ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2) as the middleware for message passing between vehicle sensors, the cloud off‑load modules and the UI. NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK provides GPU‑accelerated computation for the ACC algorithm and supports the integration of perception pipelines. Application modules are written in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for rapid prototyping of control logic. Collected vehicle telemetry, object tracks and ego‑state are persisted in a relational SQL database for later analysis and reporting. The system adheres to ISO 23247‑1 (Digital Twin framework for manufacturing) and ISO/IEC 30173 (Digital twin concepts and terminology) to ensure interoperability and standard‑compliant architecture.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires devices to share a common notion of time to support computation offloading; clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms is mandated (due to hardware limits) and is considered sufficient even though many safety‑critical vehicular standards demand tighter (<100 ms) synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin is partitioned into three top‑level entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity – each deployed on separate hardware components. Within each entity multiple Functional Entities (FE) are instantiated as ROS2 packages/nodes (e.g., Data Storage, User Interface FE split into visualization and control panel). This yields a decentralized, multi‑instance structure where functionality is distributed across hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, using an entity‑based pattern. Software components are identified, mapped to Functional Entities, and implemented as individual ROS2 packages; each package hosts one or more ROS2 nodes that execute the FE. The entities are orchestrated across separate compute nodes, providing data‑management, control, and user‑interface services while preserving ownership of resources.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The framework adheres to ISO 23247 which defines an information‑exchange layer; integration with external systems can be realized through standard protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture, although the concrete deployment is not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin System implements a Data Collection Functional Entity (FE) that gathers object tracks and the ego‑vehicle state from on‑board sensors, stores these datasets in an SQL relational database via a Data Storage FE, and provides them to a 3D Visualization FE which renders 3D object tracks, vehicle state information, and projected driving paths. The computational representations include geometric models (3‑D object tracks) and behavioral/analytical models (projected driving path based on vehicle dynamics). Model relationships are linear: sensing → data collection → database storage → visualization, enabling real‑time synchronization between physical and virtual assets. Data management follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, using entity‑based segregation of responsibilities and standard protocols such as OPC UA for data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data generated on the physical twin (LiDAR point clouds, motor encoder readings, steering servo positions, vehicle state such as speed and position) is transmitted to the digital twin. Raw LiDAR point clouds are first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which converts the raw 2‑D point representation into tracked object data. All data streams are then handled by the Data Translation functional entity, which serialises the messages, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Transmission from the physical twin to the digital twin occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. Within the digital twin, software components exchange data on a ROS 2 middleware network. The documents do not specify transmission frequency, explicit event triggers, or detailed data‑quality assurance beyond the described preprocessing and unit conversion steps, nor do they detail specific integration mechanisms beyond the Data Translation component.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin generates control commands for the physical twin, including a desired speed set‑point for the ACC algorithm, ACC enable/disable flags, and an emergency‑brake command. These commands are serialized by the Data Translation functional entity and sent back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link used for upstream sensor data. The Actuation functional entity on the physical twin receives the commands and drives the motor and steering actuators accordingly. The documents do not provide details on command message formats, validation or authentication mechanisms, feedback loops, conflict‑resolution strategies, or explicit execution acknowledgements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system employs a two‑layer network architecture. Intra‑digital‑twin software components communicate via a ROS 2 network (publish‑subscribe middleware). Inter‑entity communication between the physical twin and the digital twin uses a Wi‑Fi radio link with UDP as the transport protocol, forming a client‑server topology where the vehicle streams sensor data to the digital twin and receives control commands in return. The documents only state that the digital twin must support low end‑to‑end latency for safety‑critical ACC operation; no quantitative bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocol specifications (e.g., encryption, authentication) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: ACC computation is offloaded from the vehicle’s resource‑constrained ECU to the digital twin, which the documents indicate can be hosted on more powerful equipment such as cloud resources. The digital twin system includes services for data collection, storage, reporting, and remote management, but the specific computational (CPU, GPU) or storage capacities required are not detailed. No explicit scalability mechanisms, deployment models (edge, on‑premise, containerisation), or redundancy/availability strategies are described; only a general requirement for supporting low‑latency, safety‑critical operation is mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin implements an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) decision‑support function that first identifies the lead vehicle by clustering raw LiDAR point clouds using DBSCAN and then tracks the resulting clusters with a Kalman filter. Based on the tracked lead‑vehicle state, the Twin computes the appropriate following speed and issues the command to the vehicle controller. Analytics are therefore performed on 3‑D point‑cloud data, providing a processed representation (cluster ID, position, velocity) that feeds directly into the decision algorithm. The user can interact with the Twin via a 3‑D visualization of the environment and a control‑panel interface that displays the detected lead vehicle, the computed speed set‑point, and allows manual override. The document does not specify quantitative confidence measures for the recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system follows the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, specifically referencing Part 2 (Reference Architecture) which defines an entity‑based architecture. The architecture includes an Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), a Data Collection and Device Control Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities responsible for tasks such as actuation, data transformation, and user interfacing. The description notes that the standard does not prescribe implementation details, allowing the use of standards such as OPC Unified Architecture for communication and control. Compliance with ISO 23247 thus provides a structured, interoperable foundation for the Digital Twin.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description is highly specific and detailed, accurately reflecting the physical twin's configuration, hardware architecture (lower/upper chassis, autonomy elements), and its role as an ACC test platform. Quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle... by retrofitting it with autonomy components', 'physical architecture consists of three layers. The lower level chassis... The upper level chassis'.

Provides in-depth technical details for all three services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) including specific UI functions, the logic of the offloaded ACC algorithm, and remote control commands. It also correctly identifies the target user classes (operators and developers). Quotes: 'Data Monitoring: ... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead vehicle', 'Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed'.

The extraction correctly identifies the key technologies: ROS2 as middleware, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, Python/C++/MATLAB for modules, and SQL for data storage. It also correctly references the ISO standards. However, it incorrectly links NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 to providing computation for the ACC algorithm; the text states the ACC is offloaded to the cloud/DT, while Jetpack is the SDK for the physical twin's Jetson. Despite this minor technical inaccuracy, the extraction is rich in detail. Supported by: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink', 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction perfectly aligns with the specific temporal requirements detailed in the source. It accurately cites the 100 ms synchronization accuracy, the hardware limitation constraint, and the context of computation offloading. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.', 'Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications.'

The description accurately reflects the system's decomposition into three top-level entities, their deployment on separate hardware, and the instantiation of Functional Entities as ROS2 packages. It correctly captures the decentralized nature described. Quotes: 'User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and Physical Twin Entity', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive summary of the architecture, correctly identifying the ISO 23247 reference architecture, the entity-based pattern, the mapping of software components to ROS2 nodes, and the orchestration across compute nodes. Quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA... entity-based structure of ISO 23247.', 'map the SW components onto FEs... implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package.', 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The extraction correctly identifies the ISO 23247 information exchange layer and mentions OPC UA as a potential standard for integration, accurately noting that the standard does not mandate a specific implementation. Quotes: 'Part 4... is Information exchange.', 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA)... to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE.', 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.'

block_3_judge.reasoning: Accurately describes the data flow, functional entities (Data Collecting, Data Storage, 3D Visualization), and the specific data types (object tracks, vehicle state, projected path) stored in the SQL database. It correctly identifies the use of the ISO 23247 reference architecture and entity-based segregation. However, it inaccurately states the system uses OPC UA for data exchange ('standard protocols such as OPC UA'), whereas the text only mentions OPC UA as an example possibility within the standard while specifying ROS2 for the actual implementation. The classification of models as 'geometric' or 'behavioral' is also an inference not explicitly supported by the text. Supported by quotes: 'Data Collecting FE... Collects object tracks and ego vehicle state', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database', '3D Visualization... Presents 3D object tracks, vehicle state information, and projected driving path'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly details physical acting components. Section III.B lists a 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and describes the 'lower level chassis' controlling steering and speed. Table I explicitly maps an 'Actuation FE' that 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' and Figure 2 shows 'Motor' and 'Steering Servo' components.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text clearly describes the sensing infrastructure. Section III.B lists 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'. Figure 2 also depicts the 'LiDAR' sensor and 'Vehicle State' inputs.

The document contains minimal relevant information about model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, error detection, or quality assurance processes. While it mentions 'Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections' in the general definition and sets requirements for 'network latency of at most 100 ms' and 'clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms', these are not specific to model fidelity or validity considerations for this implementation. Since the extraction states 'Not in Document' and minimal relevant information exists, this scores 5.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements

ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).

monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_

block_4_judge.reasoning: The description accurately lists sensor data (LiDAR, motor encoder, steering servo, vehicle state) and processing steps (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Translation). It correctly identifies Wi-Fi UDP for inter-entity communication and ROS2 for internal exchange. It correctly notes the absence of frequency, event triggers, and data quality details in the source. Supported by quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds', 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U'.

The description accurately identifies control commands (speed set-point, ACC enable/disable, emergency-brake) and their path via Data Translation and the Actuation FE to motor/steering. It correctly notes the use of the same Wi-Fi UDP link and the lack of message formats, validation, or feedback details in the source. block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture
